architecture.name: ecommerce-platform-aws
architecture.description: Enterprise e-commerce platform on AWS with Spring Boot microservices, PostgreSQL RDS, and CloudFront CDN

threats.title: Session Hijacking via XSS → Token Theft
threats.description: Attacker injects malicious JavaScript via reflected/stored XSS vulnerability in React app, steals JWT tokens from localStorage, hijacks user session. No CSRF token validation allows token reuse from attacker's domain.
mitigations: Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers with nonce-based script injection protection
Use httpOnly + Secure cookies for session tokens, avoid localStorage
Implement CSRF token validation on all state-changing operations
Sanitize all user input using DOMPurify library
Regular React dependency scanning with npm audit + Snyk

threats.title: Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) on Unencrypted API Calls
threats.description: If API calls made over HTTP instead of HTTPS (misconfiguration), attacker on same network can intercept requests, extract JWT tokens, and impersonate user. No certificate pinning in React app.
mitigations: Enforce HTTPS-only communication via HSTS header (max-age >= 31536000)
Implement certificate pinning in React app for API Gateway domain
Use TLS 1.2+ with modern cipher suites
Regular security headers audit (X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, etc.)

threats.title: Credential Stuffing on Login Endpoint
threats.description: API Gateway login endpoint has no rate limiting or account lockout. Attacker uses leaked credential databases to brute-force user passwords. API Gateway logs show high 401 response rate but no alerting configured.
mitigations: Implement distributed rate limiting (10 failed attempts per IP per 15min → 5min block)
Use exponential backoff on failed login attempts
Implement CAPTCHA on 3rd failed login attempt
Monitor failed login patterns in CloudWatch, trigger alerts on 50+ failures/min
Enforce strong password policy (min 12 chars, complexity requirements)
Implement multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all user accounts

threats.title: API Request Tampering via Parameter Injection
threats.description: API Gateway does not validate request signatures. Attacker intercepts API request to /orders/123/update, modifies order quantity or price in request body, resubmits. API processes tampered request without verification.
mitigations: Implement request signing using HMAC-SHA256 (timestamp + payload hash)
Use API Gateway request/response validation models (JSON Schema)
Validate all numeric inputs have reasonable bounds (qty < 1000, price < $100k)
Implement idempotency keys for all state-changing operations
Log all request/response pairs in immutable CloudWatch logs with tamper detection

threats.title: API Version Confusion Attack
threats.description: API Gateway supports both v1 and v2 endpoints. v1 has known security bypasses (no auth check on GET /users). Attacker discovers v1 endpoint, bypasses v2 security controls, exfiltrates user data.
mitigations: Deprecate and disable all legacy API versions within 6 months
Implement versioning via Accept header + explicit version parameter
Apply identical authentication/authorization to all API versions
Log version usage in CloudWatch; alert on old version usage
Publish clear API lifecycle policy (support window = 12 months)

threats.mitre_technique_name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Cloud Logs
threats.title: Insufficient Audit Logging of API Activity
threats.description: API Gateway logs are not comprehensive. High-risk operations (user deletion, password reset, order refund) are not logged with user identity + timestamp. Attacker performs fraud, logs are incomplete, user can claim they didn't authorize it.
mitigations: Enable CloudTrail + API Gateway detailed logging for all endpoints
Log minimum: method, path, status, user_id, timestamp, request_body (redacted PII), response_status
Store logs in S3 with Object Lock (immutable 7-year retention)
Implement log integrity checking (hash each log entry)
Set up automated alerts for high-risk operations (DELETE /users, POST /refund)

threats.title: Sensitive Data Exposure in API Responses
threats.description: API returns verbose error messages with stack traces, database column names, and internal server details. /products/1 response includes cost_basis, margin, supplier_id. Attacker enumerates sensitive business data (margins, costs, supplier relationships).
mitigations: Remove all sensitive fields from API responses (cost_basis, margin, internal_id)
Implement consistent error response format: {'error': 'Operation failed', 'request_id': 'xxx'} (no stack traces)
Log full errors server-side only, never in API response
Redact PII from all API responses (email, phone, SSN)
Use API Gateway response mapping to strip sensitive headers

threats.title: Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR) in User Endpoints
threats.description: API endpoint GET /users/{userId}/orders returns all orders for userId. No authorization check. Attacker enumerates userIds 1, 2, 3, 4... and reads other users' complete order history including addresses, payment info, order totals.
mitigations: Implement explicit authorization check: verified_user_id == requested_user_id
Use UUIDs instead of sequential integer IDs to prevent enumeration
Log IDOR attempts in CloudWatch, alert on 10+ attempts from single IP
Implement API rate limiting per user (1000 requests/hour max)
Add security.txt endpoint describing bug bounty program

threats.title: Public S3 Bucket Accessible via CloudFront
threats.description: S3 bucket for product images has public read ACL. Attacker discovers bucket name in API response, accesses all product images directly from S3 (bypassing CloudFront throttling/DDoS protection). Attacker can enumerate all products and pricing.
mitigations: Set S3 bucket ACL to private; all access through CloudFront + Origin Access Identity (OAI)
Enable S3 Block Public Access (all 4 settings)
Use S3 bucket policy to deny all public access
Implement S3 server-side encryption (KMS key, not default AES256)
Enable S3 access logging to separate bucket with tamper detection

threats.title: DDoS Attack on API Gateway (Layer 7)
threats.description: Attacker sends 10,000 requests/sec to expensive endpoints (/search with complex filters, /reports/generate). No rate limiting. API Gateway processes all requests, RDS becomes CPU-bound, legitimate users experience 30sec+ response times.
mitigations: Enable AWS WAF rate limiting rule (1000 requests per 5min per IP)
Use API Gateway throttling (10,000 req/s account limit, 5,000 burst)
Implement CloudFlare DDoS protection in front of API Gateway
Cache expensive endpoints in Redis (search results, reports cached for 5min)
Monitor API Gateway request count in CloudWatch; auto-scale ECS on high load

threats.title: Slowloris Attack on HTTP Keep-Alive Connections
threats.description: Attacker opens 1,000 HTTP connections, sends partial requests very slowly (1 byte/10sec). API Gateway exhausts connection pool, legitimate users cannot connect.
mitigations: Set API Gateway timeout to 30sec (close slow connections)
Configure AWS WAF to detect slow requests (< 100 bytes/sec for > 10sec)
Use CloudFront as reverse proxy with connection limits
Monitor API Gateway connection count metric; alert on > 80% utilization

threats.title: ALB Health Check Endpoint Exploitation
threats.description: ALB health check endpoint /health returns 200 OK without authentication. Attacker discovers endpoint, uses it to redirect internal traffic, bypassing WAF rules. Or attacker modifies health check response to cause cascading failures.
mitigations: Implement authentication on health check endpoint (API key in header)
Or use private ALB health check (ALB-to-ECS via VPC only, not internet-facing)
Restrict ALB health check to specific source IPs (AWS health checker IPs)
Log all health check requests in CloudWatch, alert on 403 responses

threats.title: Credentials Stored in Environment Variables / Container Image
threats.description: ECS task definition has database credentials hardcoded in environment variables (DB_USER=admin, DB_PASS=password123). Container image includes .env file with API keys. Attacker gains access to ECS task (via EC2 compromise), reads environment, exfiltrates credentials.
mitigations: Move all credentials to AWS Secrets Manager; reference via secretsAsEnvironment in ECS task
Remove .env files from container images; use secrets manager only
Implement IAM role for ECS task with least-privilege permissions
Scan container images for secrets using GitGuardian/TruffleHog before deployment
Enable ECS task audit logging to CloudWatch; alert on secret access

threats.mitre_technique_name: Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism: Sudo and Sudo Caching
threats.title: Container Escape via Privileged Container Mode
threats.description: ECS task is configured with privileged=true (error in infrastructure setup). Attacker gains shell access to container, exploits privileged mode to mount host filesystem, modifies /etc/shadow, gains root access to underlying EC2 instance.
mitigations: Set privileged=false in all ECS task definitions
Use principle of least privilege: only grant required Linux capabilities (e.g., NET_BIND_SERVICE)
Implement AppArmor/SELinux profiles for containers
Run containers as non-root user (USER != 0 in Dockerfile)
Enable ECS task compliance scanning; fail deployment on privilege escalation risks

threats.title: Unencrypted Inter-Container Communication
threats.description: ECS tasks communicate over private VPC network without TLS. Attacker compromises one container, sniffs network traffic between containers, captures unencrypted PostgreSQL queries (including user passwords, PII in data). No network encryption implemented.
mitigations: Implement Istio service mesh with mTLS for all inter-container communication
Or use AWS App Mesh with encryption enabled
Use VPC Flow Logs to monitor inter-container traffic; alert on unencrypted connections
Implement network policies (SecurityGroups) to restrict container-to-container communication
Encrypt all RDS connections using SSL/TLS (require_secure_transport=ON)

threats.title: SQL Injection via Application
threats.description: Spring Boot service has SQL injection vulnerability in user search endpoint: SELECT * FROM users WHERE name LIKE '%' || :name || '%'. Attacker sends name=' OR '1'='1, extracts all user records including password hashes.
mitigations: Use parameterized queries exclusively (Spring Data JPA with named parameters)
Implement ORM query builder (Hibernate) to prevent SQL concatenation
Run static code analysis (SonarQube, Checkmarx) in CI/CD pipeline; fail on SQL injection risks
Apply principle of least privilege to DB user: read-only for reports, DML permissions only for specific tables
Enable PostgreSQL query logging; monitor for unusual patterns (OR '1'='1, UNION SELECT)

threats.title: Unencrypted RDS Snapshots
threats.description: RDS automated backups are stored unencrypted in S3. Attacker gains S3 access (via compromised IAM key or bucket misconfiguration), downloads RDS snapshots, extracts entire database including user passwords, credit card numbers.
mitigations: Enable RDS encryption at rest (KMS key, not AWS-managed key)
Encrypt RDS snapshots using customer-managed KMS key
Restrict snapshot sharing: uncheck 'publicly accessible', limit to specific AWS accounts
Store snapshots in private S3 bucket with encryption + versioning + MFA delete
Implement S3 access logging + CloudTrail to track who accesses snapshots

threats.title: Weak Database Credentials
threats.description: RDS master user has default password 'postgres' (never changed during setup). Attacker scans for open PostgreSQL ports (5432), brute-forces with common passwords, gains database access, exfiltrates PII data.
mitigations: Immediately rotate RDS master password to 32+ character random string
Store RDS credentials in AWS Secrets Manager with automatic rotation (every 30 days)
Use IAM database authentication instead of password-based: 'mysql -h rds-endpoint -u iamuser'
Restrict RDS security group to specific ECS task security group only (no 0.0.0.0/0)
Enable PostgreSQL password policy plugin: minimum_password_length=12, require_mixed_case, require_numbers

threats.title: Resource Exhaustion via Expensive Queries
threats.description: ECS application sends expensive SQL queries without limits: SELECT * FROM orders JOIN products JOIN users WHERE ... (no indexes). Single complex query consumes 80% RDS CPU for 30 seconds. Attacker sends 100 such queries, RDS CPU hits 100%, all other queries timeout, service becomes unavailable.
mitigations: Implement query cost analysis: set statement_timeout=5000ms in PostgreSQL
Create appropriate indexes on JOIN columns (orders.product_id, users.id)
Implement connection pooling (PgBouncer) with max_connections=50 per app instance
Monitor RDS CPU/connections in CloudWatch; alert on > 70% CPU or > 80 connections
Use Aurora Read Replicas for expensive read queries (reports, analytics)

threats.title: Redis Default Credentials
threats.description: ElastiCache Redis cluster is set up without AUTH enabled (default). Any process on VPC can connect to Redis port 6379, read/write data. Attacker compromises single ECS task, connects to Redis, reads session tokens, cart data, user preferences.
mitigations: Enable Redis AUTH with 32+ character random password
Store Redis password in Secrets Manager; reference in ECS task
Restrict ElastiCache security group to ECS task security group only
Use Redis 6.0+ with ACL support for fine-grained permissions
Enable Redis encryption in transit (TLS endpoint), encryption at rest

threats.title: Session Token Exfiltration from Redis
threats.description: Session tokens stored in Redis in plaintext (no encryption). Attacker with Redis access (via network compromise) reads KEYS session:* pattern, obtains all valid session tokens, impersonates random users.
mitigations: Encrypt session token values before storing in Redis (AES-256-GCM)
Use Redis Streams with immutability instead of simple key-value for sessions
Implement 5-minute TTL on all session keys; Redis auto-evicts expired sessions
Monitor Redis KEYS/SCAN commands in Redis logs; alert on suspicious patterns
Use Redis modules (RedisJSON, RedisSearch) with encryption for sensitive fields

threats.title: Malicious File Upload (RCE via Image Processing)
threats.description: File upload endpoint (/upload-product-image) accepts image uploads without validation. Attacker uploads malicious PHP file disguised as image.jpg. When server processes image (via ImageMagick), PHP gets executed, attacker gains RCE.
mitigations: Validate file type: check magic bytes (not just extension). Only allow image/* MIME types
Store uploaded files outside webroot, serve via CloudFront with Content-Disposition: attachment
Use S3 presigned URLs with short expiration (5min) for temporary upload access
Scan uploads with ClamAV/YARA for malware before accepting to S3
Disable S3 object executable permissions; use s3:x-amz-acl=private on all uploads

threats.title: S3 Bucket Enumeration / Subdomain Takeover
threats.description: S3 bucket name is predictable (ecommerce-media-prod-aws). Attacker performs bucket name enumeration (ecommerce-media-staging, ecommerce-media-dev, ecommerce-media-backup), finds unprotected staging bucket with test data, accesses customer PII.
mitigations: Use globally unique S3 bucket names (add random suffix: ecommerce-media-prod-k8f2h9d)
Enable S3 block public access on all buckets
Disable unneeded S3 buckets (ecommerce-media-staging); if needed, apply encryption + MFA delete
Use CloudFront URL instead of S3 direct URL in product responses
Enable S3 inventory reports to identify unused buckets; set up lifecycle policies

threats.title: Lambda Function Permission Escalation
threats.description: Lambda execution role has overly broad permissions: s3:*, dynamodb:*, logs:*. Attacker compromises Lambda function code (via dependency injection), uses Lambda's IAM role to exfiltrate data from all S3 buckets and DynamoDB tables in account.
mitigations: Apply principle of least privilege to Lambda IAM role: only s3:GetObject on ecommerce-media-prod, dynamodb:Query on specific tables
Use resource-based policies to further restrict access by ARN
Scan Lambda code dependencies for vulnerabilities (pip audit, safety, SNYK)
Enable Lambda security scanning in CodeBuild; fail deployment on high-severity CVEs
Implement Lambda code signing; only execute signed functions

threats.mitre_technique_name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Cloud Logs
threats.title: Sensitive Data Logged in CloudWatch
threats.description: Lambda functions log entire event payloads including customer data. CloudWatch logs contain unencrypted PII (names, emails, addresses). Attacker gains CloudWatch access, searches logs for sensitive data patterns (credit cards, SSNs).
mitigations: Implement centralized logging library; redact PII before logging
Use CloudWatch Logs KMS encryption for all log groups (customer-managed key)
Set IAM policy on CloudWatch Logs to require encryption in transit (AWS:SecureTransport)
Implement log retention policy: 30 days standard, 7 years for audit logs
Use CloudWatch Logs Insights to audit access to logs; alert on suspicious queries

threats.title: Message Injection in SQS Queue
threats.description: Application sends order data to SQS queue without encryption. Attacker gains access to SQS (via misconfigured bucket policy), sends malicious message: {order_id: 0, user_id: <admin>, action: 'delete_account'}. Lambda processes message, deletes admin account.
mitigations: Encrypt SQS messages server-side using KMS (SQSManagedSseEnabled=true or use KMS)
Sign all SQS messages using HMAC-SHA256; Lambda verifies signature before processing
Restrict SQS queue access via resource policy: only allow specific Lambda/ECS roles
Implement message deduplication for FIFO queues (prevent replay attacks)
Monitor SQS metrics: alert on unusual message volume or DLQ messages

threats.title: Queue Flooding Attack
threats.description: Attacker sends 100,000 messages/sec to SQS queue. Lambda concurrent executions max out (1000). Processing backlog grows unbounded. SNS topics backing Lambda have no fan-out limits. Service becomes unavailable for legitimate orders.
mitigations: Set SQS visibility timeout to 2 minutes; configure max receive count = 3 for DLQ
Implement exponential backoff in Lambda: retry delay starts at 1s, doubles each time
Configure Lambda reserved concurrency = 100 (prevent runaway scaling)
Monitor SQS ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible; alert if > 10k
Use SNS message filtering to prevent unnecessary Lambda invocations

threats.title: Overly Broad Secrets Manager IAM Permissions
threats.description: ECS task IAM role has secretsmanager:GetSecretValue on all secrets (Resource: *). Attacker gains ECS access, retrieves all secrets including payment processor API keys, database master password.
mitigations: Restrict Secrets Manager permissions by ARN: only allow specific secret names/patterns
Use resource-based policies on secrets to limit access by principal
Implement secret rotation: automatic rotation every 30 days
Enable CloudTrail logging for all Secrets Manager API calls; alert on GetSecretValue
Use resource tags to organize secrets; apply role-based access control via tag conditions

threats.mitre_technique_name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Cloud Logs
threats.title: Insufficient CloudWatch Alerting for Security Events
threats.description: Multiple failed login attempts not triggering alerts. High-risk API calls (DELETE /users, POST /refund) not generating notifications. Security team discovers breach 3 days later via audit log review (reactive not proactive).
mitigations: Create CloudWatch alarms for: 10+ failed logins/min, 50+ 403 errors/min, RDS CPU > 80%, Lambda errors > 1%
Send alarm notifications to SNS topic → PagerDuty/Slack for immediate response
Implement metric math to correlate suspicious events: (failed_logins + 403_errors) > 100 in 5min → trigger incident
Set up CloudWatch dashboard with real-time visualization of security metrics
Enable CloudTrail for all AWS API calls; stream to S3 + send to SIEM

threats.title: Lateral Movement via Compromised ECS Task
threats.description: Attacker gains shell in one ECS container (via code injection). Container is on same VPC subnet as RDS and Redis. No network segmentation. Attacker sniffs traffic between containers, discovers RDS credentials, lateral moves to database.
mitigations: Implement VPC security groups: ECS → RDS only on port 5432, ECS → Redis only on port 6379
Use network ACLs to further restrict traffic between subnets
Implement micro-segmentation using Istio/AppMesh with mTLS for container-to-container communication
Monitor VPC Flow Logs for suspicious traffic patterns (scanning, lateral movement)
Implement host-based intrusion detection (Falco) on ECS cluster

threats.title: Network ACL Misconfiguration Causes Outage
threats.description: Network ACL rule is too restrictive: deny all ephemeral ports (1024-65535). RDS connections fail due to source port restrictions. ECS → RDS communication breaks intermittently (depends on kernel port allocation). Service degradation.
mitigations: Configure network ACLs to allow ephemeral port range (1024-65535) for all outbound traffic
Test network ACL rules in staging environment before deploying to production
Use security groups instead of NACLs for most rules (security groups are stateful, NACLs are not)
Monitor VPC Flow Logs for rejected packets; alert on sustained REJECT patterns
Implement CloudWatch VPC Flow Log analysis to validate security posture

threats.title: WAF Rule Bypass via Encoding/Obfuscation
threats.description: WAF rule blocks SQL injection patterns: 'OR '1'='1. Attacker bypasses by encoding: 'OR'/**/1<>1 or using double URL encoding or case variation: SeLeCt. WAF fails to decode/normalize requests.
mitigations: Configure WAF to decode/normalize requests: URL decode, HTML decode, case normalization
Use AWS Managed Rules (AWS_CORE_RULE_SET) which include normalization
Implement WAF custom rules with regex that account for common bypasses
Monitor WAF blocked requests in CloudWatch; review blocked requests for false positives
Test WAF rules against OWASP Top 10 attack patterns before production deployment

threats.title: Cache Poisoning / HTTP Response Splitting
threats.description: CloudFront cache doesn't validate Host header. Attacker sends request with Host: attacker.com. Response is cached under attacker's domain. Other users fetch cached response, click on attacker's links, get phished.
mitigations: Set CloudFront Host header whitelist in origin custom headers
Disable CloudFront caching for dynamic content (Set-Cookie, Authorization headers)
Implement cache key normalization: use canonical Host header only
Enable CloudFront access logs; monitor for unusual Host header values

critical_areas: API Gateway security (8 threats, avg risk 12.1)
Database access control (5 threats, avg risk 11)
